events/community-health-fair.md:
The Community Health Fair offers free health screenings and flu shots for all ages. Come for blood pressure and glucose checks, vaccinations, and information on nutrition, mental health resources, and insurance enrollment—no insurance or ID required for basic services.

## Overview {#overview}

The Community Health Fair is held at the Downtown Community Center in partnership with the city health department and local hospitals. The goal is to make preventive care and health information accessible to everyone. All basic screenings and flu shots are free; no insurance or ID is required. Staff can help you understand results and refer you to follow-up care if needed.

## Screenings and services {#screenings}

- Blood pressure and glucose — Quick checks to help you track your numbers. Results are explained on the spot.
- Flu shots — Available for adults and children (parent/guardian consent for minors). No appointment needed.
- Health information — Tables on nutrition, mental health resources, and enrollment in insurance and benefit programs. Staff can help you see if you qualify for low-cost or free coverage.
- Referrals — If a screening suggests follow-up, staff can help connect you to a clinic or primary care provider and assist with scheduling.

Screenings are first-come, first-served. Wait times may vary. The fair does not replace emergency care; for emergencies, call 911.

## Location and parking {#location-parking}

The fair is at the Downtown Community Center, 456 Main Street. The building is accessible by ramp and elevator. Free parking is available in the lot next to the center; additional street parking is nearby. The center is on bus routes 2, 4, and 7. The event runs from 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM.
events/community-job-fair.md:
The Community Job Fair brings together 50+ employers hiring now. Bring your resume, dress for success, and meet recruiters from retail, healthcare, hospitality, construction, and more. Free and open to all job seekers.

## Overview {#overview}

The Community Job Fair is a free event at the Central Community Center. Employers from across the region set up tables and are ready to discuss open positions, accept resumes, and in some cases conduct on-the-spot interviews. Job coaches and workforce staff will also be on hand to help with resume tips, application questions, and referrals to training programs.

## Participating employers {#employers}

Employers typically include local hospitals, grocery stores, restaurants, construction and trades companies, childcare centers, and city contractors. A full list of participating employers is posted on the city workforce website the week before the fair and at the door the day of the event. Industries represented often include healthcare, hospitality, retail, skilled trades, and administrative support.

## How to prepare {#prepare}

- Resumes — Bring several printed copies. If you need help creating or updating a resume, visit the workforce center or library before the fair; many offer free resume workshops.
- Dress — Business casual is a good standard. You want to make a positive first impression.
- ID — Some employers may ask for ID. Bring a government-issued ID if you have one.
- Questions — Prepare a few questions to ask recruiters, such as "What does a typical day look like?" and "What are the next steps in your hiring process?"

Doors open at 9:00 AM and the fair runs until 3:00 PM. No pre-registration required; walk-ins welcome.
events/earth-day-cleanup.md:
Join neighbors for the Earth Day Community Cleanup. We'll pick up litter along the riverfront and in City Park, leaving our shared spaces cleaner and safer for everyone. All ages welcome; bring the family and wear comfortable shoes.

## Overview {#overview}

The Earth Day Community Cleanup is a volunteer event held each April. Volunteers meet at designated spots in City Park and along the riverfront, receive bags and gloves, and spend the morning cleaning up litter and debris. The city provides supplies and disposes of collected waste. No experience needed—just show up ready to help.

## What to bring {#what-to-bring}

- Clothing — Long pants and closed-toe shoes are recommended. Dress for the weather; the event runs rain or shine (light rain only; severe weather may cancel).
- Water — Bring a reusable water bottle. Water refill stations will be available at the main meetup point.
- Optional — Sunscreen, hat, and work gloves if you prefer your own (gloves are provided).

The city provides trash bags, gloves, and basic safety instructions. Minors should be accompanied by an adult or have a signed waiver from a parent or guardian.

## Meetup locations {#meetup}

- City Park — Main entrance near the fountain. Check-in and supply pickup from 8:45 AM; cleanup runs 9:00 AM–12:00 PM.
- Riverfront — Meet at the River Trail trailhead (off Oak Street). Same schedule: check-in 8:45 AM, cleanup 9:00 AM–12:00 PM.

You can choose either location when you arrive. No pre-registration required, but signing up in advance on the city website helps organizers plan supplies. After the cleanup, light refreshments are provided at the City Park check-in area.
events/senior-wellness-day.md:
Senior Wellness Day offers health screenings, nutrition tips, and connections to resources for older adults. Come for blood pressure checks, fall-prevention information, and one-on-one help with benefits and programs designed for seniors.

## Overview {#overview}

Senior Wellness Day is a free event at the North Community Center for older adults and their families. The focus is on prevention, wellness, and linking participants to ongoing services. You can get quick health checks, learn about nutrition and physical activity, and speak with staff about Medicare, SNAP, housing, and other benefits. No registration required; drop in anytime between 10:00 AM and 2:00 PM.

## Activities {#activities}

- Health screenings — Blood pressure and basic wellness checks. Results are explained on the spot; staff can refer you to a primary care provider if needed.
- Nutrition and exercise — Short talks and handouts on eating well and staying active as we age. Information on senior meal programs and fitness classes.
- Fall prevention — Tips and resources to make your home safer and reduce fall risk.
- Benefits and programs — One-on-one or small-group help with Medicare, SNAP, rental assistance, and other programs. Bring any notices or documents you have questions about.
- Resource tables — Local agencies and senior services with information and sign-up options.

Language assistance can be arranged; ask at the welcome desk. The center is wheelchair accessible.

## Location and parking {#location-parking}

The event is at the North Community Center, 200 North Avenue. Free parking is available in the lot. The center is on bus routes 5 and 11; some routes offer senior fare discounts. The event runs from 10:00 AM to 2:00 PM. Light refreshments are provided.
events/tax-help-workshop.md:
Free tax preparation assistance is available for families at the Community Center. IRS-certified volunteers can help you complete your return and claim credits you may be eligible for, such as the Earned Income Tax Credit and Child Tax Credit.

## Overview {#overview}

The Tax Preparation Help event is part of the city's free tax assistance program. Volunteers trained through the IRS Volunteer Income Tax Assistance (VITA) program help eligible households file their federal and state returns. The service is free; no fees or "refund anticipation" products. Appointments are recommended but walk-ins may be accepted as capacity allows.

## What to bring {#what-to-bring}

- ID — Government-issued photo ID for you and your spouse (if filing jointly).
- Social Security cards — For you, your spouse, and any dependents.
- Income documents — W-2s, 1099s, and any other income statements. If you're self-employed, bring records of income and expenses.
- Deductions and credits — Records for education expenses, childcare, charitable donations, etc., if you have them.
- Prior-year return — Helpful but not required.
- Bank account info — If you want your refund by direct deposit (routing and account number).

If you're missing something, come anyway; the volunteers can tell you what you need and whether you can file today or need to return with more documents.

## Appointments {#appointments}

Appointments can be made online at the city website or by calling the Community Center. Same-day walk-in slots may be available from 9:00 AM to 5:00 PM, but wait times can be long. Priority is often given to those with appointments. The event is held at the Community Center, 100 Center Street. Free parking and bus access (routes 3 and 6) are available.
events/youth-arts-showcase.md:
The Youth Arts Showcase celebrates young artists with live performances, exhibits, and hands-on activities. Bring the family to see dance, music, visual art, and more—and try your hand at creating something new.

## Overview {#overview}

The Youth Arts Showcase is a free, family-friendly event at the City Arts Center. Local youth programs, schools, and individual young artists display and perform their work. The afternoon includes a mix of scheduled performances and drop-in activities so you can stay for an hour or the whole event.

## Schedule {#schedule}

- 2:00 PM — Doors open; visual art exhibits and hands-on activity stations open.
- 2:30 PM — Welcome and first performance block (dance and music).
- 3:30 PM — Second performance block; exhibits and activities continue.
- 4:30 PM — Final performances and closing.
- 5:00 PM–6:00 PM — Mingling and last chance to visit exhibits and activities.

A detailed program with performer names and room locations is available at the door and on the Arts Center website the week of the event.

## How to participate {#participate}

As an attendee — No ticket or registration needed. Show up anytime between 2:00 PM and 6:00 PM. Seating for performances is first-come, first-served.

As a youth performer or exhibitor — Registration is handled through participating schools and youth arts programs. If you are part of a program, ask your instructor about signing up. Individual young artists can contact the Arts Center for information on limited slots for the next showcase.

The City Arts Center is at 789 Arts Way. Parking is free in the lot; the center is also on bus route 8.
news/affordable-housing-expansion.md:
City partners with developers to build mixed-income housing across five neighborhoods, with priority for families and essential workers. The Affordable Housing Expansion is the largest single commitment to affordable rental housing in the city's history and responds to years of advocacy from residents, housing nonprofits, and employers who have reported difficulty recruiting and retaining workers due to housing costs.

## Overview {#overview}

The Affordable Housing Expansion is a multi-year initiative that will create 500 new rental units for low- and moderate-income residents. The city has selected five sites for development, prioritizing neighborhoods with strong transit access and existing community services. Each development will include a mix of unit sizes to serve individuals, couples, and families; a portion of units will be set aside for households with the lowest incomes. All sites will be built to high energy-efficiency and accessibility standards, and developers have committed to local hiring and apprenticeship goals during construction.

![Affordable housing development](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1564013799919-ab600027ffc6?w=800&h=500&fit=crop "New mixed-income housing will serve families and essential workers.")

> "Every resident deserves a safe, stable place to call home. This expansion represents our commitment to inclusive growth—so that the people who teach our kids, care for our sick, and keep our city running can afford to live here."
> — Housing Director, January 2026

## Project locations {#locations}

- Northside Commons — 120 units near North Transit Station, with direct bus service to downtown and the medical district. Construction begins spring 2026; first occupancy expected late 2027. Includes a ground-floor community room and outdoor play area.
- East Gate Village — 100 units adjacent to East Community Center and within walking distance of schools and shopping. Groundbreaking fall 2026; occupancy 2028. Will include on-site social service coordination and after-school space.
- Riverside Apartments — 95 units in the Riverside neighborhood, with views of the riverfront trail and easy access to the new Route 44 bus. Pre-construction underway; construction start 2026. Units include balconies or patios; building will have a shared rooftop terrace.
- Downtown Heights — 115 units near downtown employment centers, with transit and bike-share access. Construction summer 2026; first move-ins 2028. Geared toward essential workers and families; includes bike storage and a small business incubator space on the ground floor.
- South Park Homes — 70 units near South Park and several schools. Construction spring 2027; occupancy 2029. Designed with families in mind; includes larger three-bedroom units and a playground.

![Community housing complex](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1486406146926-c627a92ad1ab?w=800&h=500&fit=crop "Units will include family-sized apartments and accessible design.")

Rent levels will be set according to area median income (AMI) and unit size; details will be published when each development opens for applications. All properties will be professionally managed with clear lease terms and maintenance procedures.

## Eligibility and application {#eligibility}

- Income limits — Households earning 30–80% of area median income may qualify, depending on the development and unit size. Specific limits (by household size) will be published for each property. Some units are reserved for households at 30–50% AMI and others for 50–80% AMI.
- Preference — Families with children, essential workers (healthcare, education, transit, and other designated sectors), and veterans receive preference in lotteries. Documentation of employment or veteran status will be required at application.
- Application — A centralized application portal opens in March 2026. Residents can sign up for alerts at housing.city.gov to be notified when each development opens for applications. Paper applications and in-person assistance will be available at city community centers and the housing office.

Units will include one-, two-, and three-bedroom options with modern amenities (e.g., in-unit laundry or building laundry, energy-efficient appliances). All developments will be transit-oriented and include community spaces. Waitlists may be long; applicants are encouraged to apply to multiple developments and to keep their contact information current.
news/city-council-infrastructure.md:
The city council has unanimously approved a comprehensive infrastructure improvement plan that will modernize key public facilities and improve transportation networks over the next five years. The vote followed months of public hearings, technical studies, and input from neighborhood associations and business groups. Council members cited the plan's balance of immediate repairs with long-term investments and its reliance on existing funding streams rather than new taxes.

## Key projects {#key-projects}

Highlights of the approved plan include:

- Road resurfacing — Over 40 miles of arterial roads will be repaved in the first two years, prioritizing corridors with the highest traffic volumes and worst pavement conditions. The program includes improved drainage and accessibility upgrades at key intersections.
- Bridge repairs — Three aging bridges (the North Avenue span, the Riverside crossing, and the East Gate overpass) will receive structural upgrades, new pedestrian walkways, and modernized lighting. Inspections and design work begin this quarter.
- Community centers — Two new multipurpose centers will open in underserved neighborhoods: one in the Northside district and one near East Gate. Each will include gym space, meeting rooms, after-school programming, and senior activities. Groundbreaking is scheduled for 2027.
- Parks and trails — Expansion of the riverfront trail by 4.5 miles, new playground equipment in five parks, and improved accessibility at entrances and restrooms. The trail extension will connect two existing segments and pass through the new Riverside Apartments development.

![Construction and development](/placeholder.jpg "Construction and development are key to the infrastructure plan.")

The city's Public Works department will lead implementation, with oversight from a new citizen advisory panel that will meet quarterly to review progress and hear community feedback.

## Timeline {#timeline}

- 2026 — Design and procurement for road and bridge projects; first paving contracts awarded by mid-year. Community center site preparation and final design.
- 2027–2028 — Major construction phases; road resurfacing continues; bridge work begins. Community centers break ground; riverfront trail expansion starts.
- 2029–2030 — Completion of remaining road and bridge projects; community centers open; new park amenities and trail segments finished. Final inspections and handover.

Residents can track progress on the city's Infrastructure Dashboard, which will be updated quarterly with photos, maps, and budget status. Sign up for project-specific email alerts at infrastructure.city.gov.

## How to get involved {#get-involved}

- Quarterly briefings — Attend public briefings at City Hall (dates posted on the city website and in the monthly newsletter). Staff present updates and answer questions; Spanish and American Sign Language interpretation available upon request.
- Email updates — Sign up for email updates on projects in your district. You can choose to receive notices only for projects within a half-mile of your address.
- Report issues — Report potholes or hazards via the 311 app or by calling 311. Reports help prioritize future resurfacing and are shared with the Infrastructure Dashboard team for inclusion in planning.

![City Hall and civic engagement](/placeholder.jpg "City Hall hosts public briefings on infrastructure progress.")

Funding for the plan is secured through a combination of federal grants, state matching funds, and existing capital budgets. No new general tax increase is required for this phase. The city will apply for additional federal funding in 2026 and 2027 to accelerate certain projects if grants are awarded.
news/community-health-initiative.md:
A new health and wellness program aims to improve access to healthcare services for all residents through mobile clinics, telemedicine options, and monthly health fairs. The Community Health Initiative is the result of a two-year planning process that brought together the city health department, local hospitals, community health centers, and nonprofit organizations. The goal is to reduce barriers to preventive care and same-day advice, especially in neighborhoods where primary care has been limited and chronic disease rates are high.

## Overview {#overview}

The Community Health Initiative launches this month in partnership with local hospitals, community health centers, and nonprofit organizations. The program focuses on prevention, screenings, and same-day care in neighborhoods where access to primary care has been limited. Initial funding covers 18 months of operations, with a commitment from partners to seek sustainable funding to make the program permanent and expand hours and locations based on demand.

![Healthcare and community wellness](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1576091160399-112ba8d25d1d?w=800&h=500&fit=crop "Mobile clinics and telemedicine are central to the initiative.")

> "Everyone deserves access to quality healthcare. This initiative meets people where they are—in their neighborhoods and online. We're not replacing primary care; we're bridging the gap until everyone has a medical home."
> — Health Commissioner, January 2026

## Services offered {#services}

The initiative provides:

- Mobile clinics — Vans equipped for basic exams, vaccinations (flu, COVID-19, routine adult), and blood pressure and glucose screenings. Stops at libraries, community centers, and senior housing on a rotating schedule. No appointment required for screenings and vaccinations; walk-ups welcome. Staff can refer participants to primary care and specialty services and help with scheduling and paperwork.
- Telemedicine — Free video visits with nurses and clinicians for non-urgent issues such as colds, minor injuries, medication questions, and mental health check-ins. Available in multiple languages (Spanish, Mandarin, and others via interpreter); appointments can be booked online or by phone. Same-day and next-day slots are often available.
- Health fairs — Monthly events with screenings (blood pressure, glucose, cholesterol by partner organizations), flu shots, and information on nutrition, mental health resources, and enrollment in insurance and benefit programs. Fairs are held at different locations each month to reach a wide audience.
- Referrals — Staff help connect residents to primary care providers, specialty care, and social services when needed. Follow-up calls are made when possible to ensure residents were able to access referred care.

![Mobile health van and outreach](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1579684385127-1ef15d508118?w=800&h=500&fit=crop "Mobile clinics bring basic care to underserved areas.")

The initiative does not provide emergency care. Residents with life-threatening emergencies should call 911 or go to the nearest emergency room.

## Locations and hours {#locations}

- Mobile clinic schedule — Posted weekly on the city health department website and at partner sites (libraries, community centers). The schedule is also available by calling the health department hotline. No appointment required for screenings and vaccinations; wait times vary by site and time of day.
- Telemedicine — Available Monday–Friday, 8:00 a.m. – 6:00 p.m. Same-day and next-day slots are often available; book online at health.city.gov/telemedicine or by phone. You will need a device with a camera and internet connection; staff can help arrange access at a library or community center if needed.
- Health fairs — Dates and locations are announced at the start of each month on the health department website, in the city newsletter, and on flyers at partner sites.

![Community health event](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1551076805-e1869033e561?w=800&h=500&fit=crop "Health fairs offer screenings and connections to care.")

## Eligibility {#eligibility}

- Mobile clinics and health fairs — Open to all city residents. No insurance or ID required for basic screenings and vaccinations. Staff may ask for name and contact information for follow-up and to help with referrals; providing this information is optional for screenings and vaccinations.
- Telemedicine — Available to residents who live in the city and do not have a regular provider or need same-day advice. Insurance is not required; sliding-scale fees may apply for some follow-up services (e.g., certain lab work or specialist referrals). Minors must have consent from a parent or guardian for telemedicine visits.

Interpretation services are available for major languages; request when booking or at the mobile clinic. American Sign Language interpretation can be arranged for telemedicine with advance notice.

The initiative is funded through a mix of city funds, federal grants, and hospital community benefit commitments. The goal is to make the program permanent and expand hours and locations based on demand and ongoing evaluation.
news/free-job-training-program-2026.md:
Residents can now sign up for certification programs in healthcare, technology, and skilled trades—all tuition-free with childcare support. The City Job Training Program has trained over 2,000 residents since its launch five years ago, with strong job placement rates and employer partnerships. The 2026 cohort expands offerings in high-demand fields and adds new options for childcare and transportation so that more people can participate.

## Overview {#overview}

The City Job Training Program opens enrollment for 2026 with expanded offerings in high-demand fields. All programs are tuition-free for city residents, and eligible participants can receive childcare vouchers and transportation assistance (e.g., transit passes or gas cards for those who must drive). Programs combine classroom instruction with hands-on training and, where applicable, industry certifications that employers recognize. Many programs include internships or job shadows with local employers, and job placement assistance is available upon completion.

![Job training and education](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1523240795612-9a054b0db644?w=800&h=500&fit=crop "Certification programs prepare residents for in-demand careers.")

> "Skills open doors. We're investing in our residents so they can compete for family-sustaining jobs. Our employer partners tell us they need more trained workers—this program is the bridge."
> — Workforce Development Director, January 2026

## Available programs {#programs}

- Healthcare — Certified Nursing Assistant (CNA), Medical Assistant, Pharmacy Technician. Program length 8–16 weeks depending on credential. Includes clinical rotations at local hospitals and clinics. Strong demand from healthcare employers; many graduates receive job offers before completion.
- Technology — IT Support, Data Analytics, Web Development. 12–24 weeks. Combines technical skills with soft skills (communication, problem-solving). Some cohorts offer flexible evening or hybrid options for working adults.
- Skilled Trades — Electrical, Plumbing, HVAC, Construction. 6–12 months. Hands-on training in labs and on project sites; apprenticeship readiness and safety certifications included. Industry partners provide tools and mentorship.
- Business — Business Administration, Bookkeeping, Customer Service. 6–10 weeks. Geared toward office and retail roles; includes resume and interview preparation. Some participants use these skills to start or improve a small business.

![Adult education classroom](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1509062522246-3755977927d7?w=800&h=500&fit=crop "Hands-on training and industry partnerships ensure job readiness.")

Program schedules (full-time, part-time, day, evening) vary by program and cohort. Info sessions cover schedule options and help applicants choose a program that fits their situation.

## How to apply {#apply}

1. Eligibility — Must be a city resident, 18 or older. Some programs require a high school diploma or equivalent (GED); others do not. Eligibility for childcare and transportation support is based on income and household size; staff will explain at info sessions.
2. Info sessions — Monthly virtual and in-person sessions explain each program, schedule, and support options and answer questions. Attendance at one session is required before applying. Sessions are listed at workforce.city.gov and announced by email if you sign up for the program newsletter.
3. Application — Apply online at workforce.city.gov or visit any city community center for assistance. You will need to provide proof of residency and, for some programs, education history. Application does not guarantee enrollment; programs fill quickly.
4. Enrollment — Cohorts start quarterly (January, April, July, October). Early application is recommended due to high demand. Accepted applicants will receive orientation and start dates by email and phone.

Childcare and transportation support are available for qualifying participants. Programs include job placement assistance upon completion, including resume help, interview practice, and connections to hiring employers. Graduates also have access to alumni networks and continued career coaching for one year after completion.
news/public-transit-routes-spring.md:
Enhanced public transportation services will connect underserved neighborhoods starting next month, with three new bus routes and extended service hours across the city. The expansion is the largest single service increase in over a decade and reflects the transit authority's commitment to equity and climate goals. Ridership on pilot corridors grew by 18% during the trial period, with strong support from residents and employers.

## Overview {#overview}

The transit authority announced the expansion after a year-long pilot and community feedback from thousands of riders, neighborhood meetings, and an online survey. The new routes fill gaps in the current network and align with the city's goal of reducing car dependency and improving access to jobs, schools, healthcare, and services. The authority also cited partnerships with major employers along the new corridors, several of which are expanding transit benefit programs for employees.

![Public transit bus in the city](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1544620347-c4fd4a3d5957?w=800&h=500&fit=crop "New bus routes will serve more neighborhoods.")

> "Reliable transit is essential for our community. These routes are a direct response to what residents told us they need—more frequent service, better connections, and later hours for shift workers and evening activities."
> — Transit Director, January 2026

## New routes {#new-routes}

Three new bus routes will launch in March 2026:

- Route 42 — North Corridor — Connects the northern neighborhoods to the downtown hub and medical district. Stops include the new community center, two major employers, North Library, and Northside Commons (the upcoming affordable housing development). Expected to serve an estimated 8,000 riders per week.
- Route 43 — East–West Express — Links the east side to the west side with limited stops for faster commutes. Peak-hour service every 15 minutes; midday and evening service every 30 minutes. Key stops include East Gate Village, the arts district, and the west-side industrial park.
- Route 44 — Riverfront Loop — A circular route along the riverfront, serving parks, housing, and the arts district. Ideal for weekend and evening travel, with connections to the expanded riverfront trail. Runs every 20 minutes on weekends and every 30 minutes on weekdays.

![Transit map and planning](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1477959858617-67f85cf4f1df?w=800&h=500&fit=crop "Transit planning supports new routes and extended hours.")

Real-time arrival signs will be installed at the busiest stops on all three routes by the end of 2026. The authority is also evaluating additional shelters and seating based on rider feedback during the first six months.

## Schedule and hours {#schedule}

- Weekday service on all three routes: 5:30 a.m. – 10:00 p.m. Route 42 and 43 offer peak-hour frequency of every 12–15 minutes.
- Saturday and Sunday on Route 42 and 44: 7:00 a.m. – 8:00 p.m.; Route 43 runs weekday-only initially, with weekend service under consideration for 2027.
- Extended hours on existing high-ridership routes: 12 additional routes will run until 11:00 p.m. on weekdays (previously 9:00 or 9:30 p.m.), allowing more flexibility for evening work, education, and recreation.

Schedules and real-time arrival information will be available on the Transit app, on the authority's website, and at major stops. Printed schedules will be distributed at libraries and community centers in the weeks before launch.

## Feedback {#feedback}

Riders can share feedback in several ways:

- Surveys — Short in-app and on-board surveys will run during the first six months to gather data on trip purpose, frequency, and satisfaction. Participants can opt in to a quarterly drawing for free monthly passes.
- Public meetings — Quarterly open houses will be held to discuss ridership, on-time performance, and possible adjustments to schedules or stops. Dates and locations are posted on the transit authority website.
- 311 — Report issues (e.g., missed runs, accessibility concerns, safety) or suggestions by phone or the 311 app. Reports are tracked and used in service planning.

![Bus stop and rider experience](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1570125909232-eb263c188f7e?w=800&h=500&fit=crop "New and improved bus stops support the expanded network.")

Fares remain unchanged. Youth, seniors, and eligible low-income riders continue to qualify for reduced or free fares through existing programs. The authority is also piloting a fare-capping program on two routes in 2026, so that riders who take multiple trips per day never pay more than the cost of a day pass.
news/winter-weather-resource-guide.md:
Extended hours at city warming centers and additional outreach teams will help residents stay safe during cold weather this season. The city has coordinated with shelters, libraries, recreation centers, and outreach organizations to ensure that everyone has a place to get warm and that vulnerable residents—including those experiencing homelessness—are connected to resources and transport. Residents are encouraged to share this information with neighbors who may need assistance and to call 311 if they see someone in distress during cold weather.

## Overview {#overview}

As winter continues, the city has expanded warming center hours and deployed extra outreach teams to connect vulnerable residents with shelter and resources. Cold weather alerts are issued when temperatures fall below 32°F or when wind chill creates dangerous conditions; during alerts, additional sites extend hours and free transportation to warming centers is available. All city residents are encouraged to sign up for alerts at weather.city.gov and to share warming center and shelter information with friends, family, and neighbors.

![Winter safety and warming](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1491002052546-bf38f186af56?w=800&h=500&fit=crop "Warming centers provide safe spaces during cold weather.")

> "No one should be left out in the cold. Our warming centers and outreach teams are here to help. If you need a warm place or know someone who does, please reach out."
> — Emergency Services Coordinator, January 2026

## Locations and hours {#locations}

- Central Community Center — 123 Main St. Open 24/7 during cold alerts (under 32°F or dangerous wind chill). When no alert is active, open during regular building hours. Hot drinks, restrooms, and seating; information on permanent housing and shelter referrals available from staff.
- North Library — 456 Oak Ave. Open 8 a.m.–10 p.m. daily. Extended to midnight during cold alerts. Restrooms and seating; no overnight stays. Patrons are welcome to read, use computers, or rest during open hours.
- East Recreation Center — 789 Pine St. Open 6 a.m.–10 p.m. weekdays, 8 a.m.–8 p.m. weekends. Extended hours during cold alerts. Gym and lobby available for warming; hot drinks sometimes provided by volunteers.
- South Shelter — 321 Elm Blvd. Open 24/7. Overnight stays available; meals provided. This is a full overnight shelter with beds and basic services. Capacity is limited; outreach teams can help with intake and transport.
- West Warming Site — 654 Maple Dr. Open 4 p.m.–8 a.m. during cold alerts (overnight focus). Hot drinks, restrooms, and seating; staff can arrange transport to overnight shelter if needed.

![Community center interior](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1557804506-669a67965ba0?w=800&h=500&fit=crop "Warm spaces, hot drinks, and information on permanent housing resources.")

Sites may adjust hours during holidays or severe weather; check weather.city.gov or call 311 for the latest. Pets are not allowed at most warming centers; South Shelter has limited space for pets—call ahead to ask about availability.

## Transportation {#transportation}

- Free rides — Call 311 during cold alerts for free transit to warming centers. Service is available 24/7 when alerts are active. Rides are provided to and from designated warming centers and shelters. Wait times may vary during peak demand.
- Outreach vans — Street outreach teams patrol downtown and known encampment areas during cold weather. They provide warm blankets, hot drinks, and transport to shelters and warming centers. If you see someone who may need help, you can call 311 to request an outreach check (provide location and, if safe, a description).
- Transit passes — Temporary day passes are available at warming centers for those who need to reach other locations (e.g., work, medical appointments). Ask staff at the front desk or information table.

Cold weather alerts are issued when temperatures fall below 32°F or wind chill creates dangerous conditions. Alerts are posted on weather.city.gov, sent to subscribers by email and text, and announced on the city’s social media. Sign up at weather.city.gov to receive alerts and reminders about warming center hours and transportation.
